section.title: Nicodemus and the New Birth
section.sectionRationale: A leading Pharisee comes to Jesus by night, and Jesus meets his cautious admiration with a demand that cannot be met by effort: you must be born again. The conversation opens out into the Gospel's most famous words, that God so loved the world He gave His only Son. The chapter closes with John the Baptist's last, self-effacing testimony, He must increase, I must decrease.
section.thesis: Entry into the kingdom of God is not by human descent, effort, or learning but by a birth from above, worked by the Spirit, and grounded in the Son of Man lifted up like the serpent in the wilderness, so that everyone who believes in the Son whom God gave in love will not perish but have eternal life.
section.frameNote: Four movements: Nicodemus and the necessity of the new birth from above; the earthly and heavenly witness and the Son of Man lifted up; God's love in giving His Son and the verdict of light and darkness; and John the Baptist's final testimony that Christ must increase.

units.label: You must be born again
units.note: the new birth by the Spirit

verses.text: Now there was a man of the Pharisees named Nicodemus, a leader of the Jews.
He came to Jesus at night and said, “Rabbi, we know that You are a teacher who has come from God. For no one could perform the signs You are doing if God were not with him.”
Jesus replied, “Truly, truly, I tell you, no one can see the kingdom of God unless he is {{anothen|born again}}.”
“How can a man be born when he is old?” Nicodemus asked. “Can he enter his mother’s womb a second time to be born?”
Jesus answered, “Truly, truly, I tell you, no one can enter the kingdom of God unless he is born of water and the {{pneuma|Spirit}}.
Flesh is born of flesh, but spirit is born of the Spirit.
Do not be amazed that I said, ‘You must be born again.’
The wind blows where it wishes. You hear its sound, but you do not know where it comes from or where it is going. So it is with everyone born of the Spirit.”

units.label: Earthly and heavenly things
units.note: the Son of Man must be lifted up

verses.text: “How can this be?” Nicodemus asked.
“You are Israel’s teacher,” said Jesus, “and you do not understand these things?
Truly, truly, I tell you, we speak of what we know, and we testify to what we have seen, and yet you people do not accept our testimony.
If I have told you about earthly things and you do not believe, how will you believe if I tell you about heavenly things?
No one has ascended into heaven except the One who descended from heaven, the Son of Man.
Just as Moses {{hupsoo|lifted up}} the snake in the wilderness, so the Son of Man must be lifted up,
that everyone who believes in Him may have eternal life.

units.label: God so loved the world
units.note: the verdict of light and darkness

verses.text: For God so {{agapao|loved}} the world that He gave His one and only Son, that everyone who believes in Him shall not perish but have {{zoe|eternal life}}.
For God did not send His Son into the world to condemn the world, but to save the world through Him.
Whoever believes in Him is not condemned, but whoever does not believe has already been condemned, because he has not believed in the name of God’s one and only Son.
And this is the {{krisis|verdict}}: The Light has come into the world, but men loved the darkness rather than the Light because their deeds were evil.
Everyone who does evil hates the Light, and does not come into the Light for fear that his deeds will be exposed.
But whoever practices the truth comes into the Light, so that it may be seen clearly that what he has done has been accomplished in God.”
After this, Jesus and His disciples went into the Judean countryside, where He spent some time with them and baptized.
Now John was also baptizing at Aenon near Salim, because the water was plentiful there, and people kept coming to be baptized.
(For John had not yet been thrown into prison.)
Then a dispute arose between John’s disciples and a certain Jew over the issue of ceremonial washing.
So John’s disciples came to him and said, “Look, Rabbi, the One who was with you beyond the Jordan, the One you testified about, He is baptizing, and everyone is going to Him.”
John replied, “A man can receive only what is given him from heaven.
You yourselves can testify that I said, ‘I am not the Christ, but am sent ahead of Him.’
The bride belongs to the bridegroom. The friend of the bridegroom stands and listens for him, and is overjoyed to hear the bridegroom’s voice. That joy is mine, and it is now complete.
He must increase; I must decrease.
The One who comes from above is above all. The one who is from the earth belongs to the earth and speaks as one from the earth. The One who comes from heaven is above all.
He testifies to what He has seen and heard, yet no one accepts His testimony.
Whoever accepts His testimony has certified that God is truthful.
For the One whom God has sent speaks the words of God, for God gives the Spirit without limit.
The Father loves the Son and has placed all things in His hands.
Whoever believes in the Son has eternal life. Whoever rejects the Son will not see life. Instead, the wrath of God remains on him.”

anothen.gloss: Again, anew, or from above. The word carries both senses at once, and the double meaning drives the conversation: Nicodemus hears born again and thinks of a second physical birth, but Jesus means born from above, a birth that only God can give. The new birth is not self-improvement but a fresh origin from heaven.

pneuma.gloss: Spirit, wind, or breath, the same word for all three. Jesus exploits the overlap: the wind blows where it wishes, unseen and sovereign, and so it is with everyone born of the Spirit. The new birth is the sovereign, mysterious work of God's Spirit, who gives life where and as He wills.

hupsoo.gloss: To lift up, raise high, exalt. In John the word has a deliberate double sense: Jesus will be physically lifted up on the cross and thereby lifted up in glory. As Moses raised the bronze serpent so the bitten might look and live, the Son of Man is lifted up so that all who look to Him in faith may have eternal life.

agapao.gloss: To love, the verb of the noun agape, a love that gives and seeks the good of another. God so loved the world that He gave His Son; this is love defined by costly self-giving, not mere sentiment. The measure of the love is the gift, the one and only Son, and its aim is that believers should not perish but live.

zoe.gloss: Life, specifically eternal life, the life of the age to come and the very life of God. In John eternal life is not only endless duration but a quality of life that begins now in knowing the Father and the Son. It is the gift given to everyone who believes in the Son who was lifted up.

krisis.gloss: Judgment, verdict, decision. The coming of the Light is itself the verdict; people are judged by whether they come to the Light or hide from it. Jesus came not to condemn but to save, yet the response to Him divides humanity, and unbelief is self-condemnation, a preferring of darkness to light.

basic.context: Nicodemus, a respected Pharisee and teacher, comes to Jesus under cover of darkness with a careful compliment. Jesus cuts past it to the heart of the matter: no one can even see God's kingdom unless he is born again, born from above. Nicodemus is baffled, and Jesus explains that this birth is the work of the Spirit, as free and mysterious as the wind. Then comes the heart of the Gospel: God so loved the world that He gave His only Son. The chapter ends with John the Baptist gladly stepping into the background so that Jesus can take center stage.
basic.hermeneutics: The key questions are, how does anyone enter God's kingdom, and what has God done about our need. Jesus answers that entry is by new birth, which we cannot produce; it is God's gift by the Spirit. And the ground of that gift is the Son lifted up on the cross, given out of God's love. Notice the play on words, born again and from above, wind and Spirit, lifted up on a cross and in glory. Faith is looking to the lifted-up Son, as Israel looked to the bronze serpent and lived.
basic.originalLanguages: The dialogue turns on double meanings. Anothen means both again and from above; Jesus means a birth from heaven. Pneuma means both wind and Spirit; the new birth is as sovereign and unseen as the wind. Hupsoo, lifted up, points to both the cross and the exaltation. God's agape love gives the Son so that believers may have zoe, eternal life, and the coming of the Light is itself the krisis, the verdict.
basic.typology: Moses lifting up the bronze serpent so that the dying might look and live is a direct picture of Christ lifted up on the cross, that whoever looks to Him in faith may live. The new birth by water and the Spirit fulfills the prophets' promise of cleansing and a new heart.
basic.christ: Christ is the One who came down from heaven and will be lifted up on the cross. He is the only Son whom God gave in love, the Light that has come into the world, the bridegroom whose joy John shares. To believe in Him is to have eternal life; to reject Him is to remain under wrath.
basic.god: God is love, and His love is not passive; it gives at the highest cost, His one and only Son. He sends the Son not to condemn but to save. He is also holy, so that the rejection of His Son leaves a person under judgment. And He gives the Spirit who alone can bring the new birth.
basic.crossReferences: Numbers 21 tells of the bronze serpent lifted up. Ezekiel 36 promises cleansing with water and a new spirit. 1 Peter 1 speaks of being born again to a living hope. Ephesians 2 describes being made alive by grace.
basic.oneStory: Humanity, fallen and unable to save itself, needs a birth it cannot achieve. In love God gives His Son to be lifted up like the serpent in the wilderness, so that all who look to Him live. The Spirit brings the new birth the prophets promised, and the whole story turns on whether we come to the Light or hide in the dark.

context.literary: A night visit that becomes a discourse, moving from misunderstanding to revelation, a characteristic Johannine pattern. Nicodemus fades from view as Jesus' words open into the great summary of verses 16 to 21, then the scene shifts to John the Baptist's parallel testimony, which echoes the same themes of heaven and earth, testimony, belief, and life. The famous verse 16 sits at the hinge, and interpreters differ on where Jesus' words end and the narrator's begin.
context.historical: Nicodemus is a member of the ruling council, a Pharisee steeped in the law, which sharpens the shock that even Israel's teacher must be born again. Coming by night suggests caution and perhaps the darkness and light motif at work. Ceremonial washing was a live concern among Jews and John's disciples. John baptized at Aenon near Salim where water was plentiful, and the note that he was not yet imprisoned situates the scene early in the ministry.
context.canonical: The new birth links to the prophetic promises of Ezekiel 36 and to the New Testament's teaching on regeneration in Titus 3:5 and 1 Peter 1:3. The bronze serpent of Numbers 21 is here given its fulfillment. John 3:16 stands as perhaps the most concentrated statement of the gospel in Scripture, holding together God's love, the gift of the Son, the necessity of faith, and the alternatives of perishing or eternal life.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that entrance into God's kingdom requires a new birth that only God's Spirit can give, to ground salvation in the Son lifted up out of God's love, and to call readers to believe in the Son and so have eternal life rather than remain in darkness under wrath.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes a particular conversation, but its teaching is prescriptive and universal: everyone must be born again, and everyone is called to believe in the Son who was lifted up. John the Baptist's He must increase models the abiding posture of every witness.

debates.question: What is meant by born of water and the Spirit in verse 5?

views.label: Spiritual cleansing and renewal
views.case: Water and Spirit together echo Ezekiel 36:25-27, where God sprinkles clean water and puts a new spirit within; the phrase describes one reality, the cleansing, renewing new birth God works, fitting the flesh and spirit contrast that follows.

views.label: Baptism and the Spirit
views.case: Some take water as a reference to baptism, whether John's or Christian baptism, joined to the Spirit's inward work; the outward sign and inward reality of new birth belong together.

debates.bearing: Either way the emphasis falls on a birth from above that God alone gives; it is not natural birth or human effort but the cleansing, life-giving work of the Spirit.

debates.question: Where do Jesus' words end and the narrator's comment begin in this chapter?

views.label: Jesus speaks through verse 21
views.case: The words flow naturally as continued speech to Nicodemus, and the BSB keeps the quotation open to verse 21; the great summary is Jesus' own teaching.

views.label: The narrator comments from verse 16
views.case: Some translators close the quotation after verse 15 and read verses 16 to 21 as John's inspired reflection; the third-person reference to God's one and only Son can suggest the evangelist's voice.

debates.bearing: The meaning is unaffected either way; whether spoken by Jesus or by the inspired narrator, verses 16 to 21 are the Gospel's own summary of God's saving love.

study.originalLanguages: The conversation is built on words that carry two senses. Jesus tells Nicodemus he must be born anothen, both again and from above, and Nicodemus stumbles on the physical meaning while Jesus intends the heavenly one. The new birth is the work of the pneuma, the Spirit, which is also the word for wind: unseen, sovereign, blowing where it wills. The Son of Man must be hupsoo, lifted up, on the cross and into glory, as Moses lifted the serpent. God's agape, His self-giving love, gives the Son so that believers may have zoe, eternal life. And the coming of the Light is the krisis, the verdict, that divides those who come to the Light from those who hide in the dark.

typology.type: The bronze serpent lifted up in the wilderness, Numbers 21:8-9
typology.fulfillment: The Son of Man lifted up on the cross, so that everyone who looks to Him in faith may live, John 3:14-15.

typology.type: The prophetic promise of clean water and a new spirit, Ezekiel 36:25-27
typology.fulfillment: The new birth of water and the Spirit that Jesus says is required to enter the kingdom.
Christ the bridegroom who claims the bride, with John the Baptist as the joyful friend who steps aside, John 3:29.

study.christ: Christ stands at the center as the One who descended from heaven and must be lifted up. He is the fulfillment of the bronze serpent: as the dying Israelites looked to the pole and lived, so all who look to the crucified Son in faith have eternal life. He is the one and only Son whom God gave in love, sent not to condemn but to save. He is the Light come into the world and the bridegroom whose voice brings joy. The Father loves Him and has placed all things in His hands, so that to have the Son is to have life, and to reject Him is to remain under wrath.
study.god: The chapter unveils the heart of God. God so loved the world that He gave His one and only Son; His love is measured by the costliness of the gift. He sent the Son not to condemn but to save, revealing a God whose deepest desire is rescue, not destruction. Yet He is holy and just: those who refuse the Son remain under His wrath, and the coming of the Light exposes the human preference for darkness. God is the giver of the Spirit without limit and the author of the new birth no one can produce for himself.

crossReferences.note: Moses lifts up the bronze serpent, and all who look to it live, the type Jesus applies to His own lifting up.
God promises clean water and a new spirit, the background to being born of water and the Spirit.
God has caused us to be born again to a living hope through the resurrection of Jesus Christ.
He saved us by the washing of regeneration and renewal by the Holy Spirit, echoing the new birth.

study.oneStory: The chapter gathers the whole gospel into a single scene. Humanity is fallen and cannot save itself; it needs a birth from above that only God's Spirit gives. In love the Father gives His one and only Son to be lifted up like the serpent in the wilderness, so that everyone who looks to Him in faith may live. The Spirit brings the new birth the prophets promised, and the coming of the Light forces the great decision of the story: to come to the Light and live, or to hide in the dark. John the Baptist shows the fitting response, He must increase, I must decrease.

quiz.q: What does Jesus tell Nicodemus is necessary to see the kingdom of God?

options.text: To be born again, born from above by the Spirit.
options.note: Verses 3 to 8 make the new birth from above by the Spirit the requirement.

options.text: To keep the law more carefully than the Pharisees.
options.note: Jesus says even Israel's teacher must be born again; law-keeping cannot achieve it.

options.text: To be cleansed and renewed inwardly by God.
options.note: True to the meaning of new birth, but it stops short of naming the birth from above by the Spirit.

options.text: To understand heavenly things through careful study.
options.note: Nicodemus was a learned teacher, yet study did not suffice; the need is new birth, not more learning.

quiz.q: What does the lifting up of the Son of Man like the bronze serpent point to?

options.text: Christ lifted up on the cross, so that all who look to Him in faith have eternal life.
options.note: Verses 14 and 15 apply Numbers 21 to Christ crucified and the life given to believers.

options.text: Christ ascending into heaven at the end of His ministry.
options.note: The lifting up in John refers first to the cross, not chiefly the ascension.

options.text: Christ being exalted and glorified through His death.
options.note: True, since lifting up carries the sense of exaltation, but the primary reference is the cross where He is lifted up.

options.text: Christ raising a physical banner over Israel.
options.note: The image is the serpent on the pole fulfilled in the crucifixion, not a military banner.

quiz.q: According to John 3:16, why did God give His one and only Son?

options.text: Because He loved the world, so that everyone who believes should not perish but have eternal life.
options.note: Verse 16 grounds the gift of the Son in God's love and its aim in eternal life for believers.

options.text: To condemn a sinful world for its rebellion.
options.note: Verse 17 says the opposite; God sent the Son to save the world, not to condemn it.

options.text: So that those who believe in Him would have eternal life.
options.note: True and central, but it leaves out that the gift flows from God's love for the world.

options.text: To reward those who already loved Him.
options.note: The love is God's initiative toward a world in darkness, not a reward for prior human love.

quiz.q: What is the verdict Jesus describes in verses 19 to 21?

options.text: The Light has come, but people loved the darkness because their deeds were evil.
options.note: Verse 19 defines the verdict as the response to the Light that has come.

options.text: God has already destroyed everyone who sins.
options.note: The verdict is about response to the Light; Jesus came to save, not to destroy at once.

options.text: People are judged by whether they come to the Light or hide from it.
options.note: A fair summary, but it omits the reason given, that people loved darkness because their deeds were evil.

options.text: Everyone will be judged only at the last day.
options.note: There is a final judgment, but here the verdict is already being rendered in how people respond to the Light now.

quiz.q: How does John the Baptist respond when told everyone is going to Jesus?

options.text: With joy, saying He must increase and I must decrease, for the bride belongs to the bridegroom.
options.note: Verses 29 and 30 show John's glad self-effacement before Christ.

options.text: With jealousy that his own following is shrinking.
options.note: The opposite; John rejoices to hear the bridegroom's voice.

options.text: By reminding them he is only the friend of the bridegroom, not the Christ.
options.note: True to his words, but it misses the joyful heart of his response, that Christ must increase.

options.text: By quietly withdrawing without explanation.
options.note: John does step back, but he explains his joy clearly rather than withdrawing in silence.

quiz.q: What does the chapter say is true of the one who rejects the Son?

options.text: He will not see life; the wrath of God remains on him.
options.note: Verse 36 states this plainly alongside the promise of life to those who believe.

options.text: He is given more time and no consequences follow.
options.note: Verse 36 says the wrath of God remains on him; rejection has grave consequences.

options.text: He remains under judgment until he believes.
options.note: True in substance, but softer than the text, which says the wrath of God remains on him.

options.text: He simply misses out on some extra blessings.
options.note: It is not a minor loss; verse 36 speaks of not seeing life and remaining under wrath.

questions: Jesus told a religious, moral, learned man that he still needed to be born again. Where are you tempted to trust your own goodness or knowledge instead of the new birth only God can give?
The new birth is like the wind, God's free and sovereign work. How does it comfort you, or unsettle you, that you cannot manufacture spiritual life but must receive it?
As the bitten Israelites looked to the serpent and lived, we look to Christ lifted up. What does it mean for you to simply look to Him in faith rather than trying to fix yourself first?
God so loved the world that He gave His Son. How does the costliness of that gift reshape the way you think about God's love for you?
John the Baptist said He must increase, I must decrease. In what part of your life is God calling you to step back so that Christ can be seen more clearly?

translationNotes.note: Born again renders anothen; the BSB footnote gives or born from above, also in verse 7. The word holds both senses, and Nicodemus's confusion turns on hearing only again.
The wind blows and everyone born of the Spirit uses one Greek word, pneuma, for both wind and Spirit; the wordplay pictures the sovereign, unseen work of the Spirit.
The BSB footnote notes that some manuscripts (BYZ and TR) add who is in heaven at the end of the verse; the sense of the Son of Man's heavenly origin is unchanged.
One and only renders monogenes; the BSB footnote gives or only begotten or unique, also in verse 18. The emphasis is on the Son's unique relation to the Father.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for John 3:1-36. Verse text assembled by concatenating content segments in order; footnote markers and line breaks skipped. Em dashes in verses 13 (heaven, the Son of Man) and 26 (testified about, He is baptizing) were converted to commas. No em or en dashes remain. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
